diagrams.title: Pumping Lemma Visualization
diagrams.description: A diagram illustrating the Pumping Lemma concept showing how any sufficiently long string in a regular language can be divided into three parts (xyz), where the middle part y can be 'pumped' (repeated) any number of times while the resulting string still belongs to the language.
elements: Division into three parts: x, y, z
Arrows showing y can be repeated 0 or more times
diagrams.purpose: Explains the formal statement of the Pumping Lemma for regular languages and how it establishes a necessary condition for regularity that can be used to prove languages are non-regular

diagrams.title: Kleene's Theorem Equivalence
diagrams.description: A diagram showing Kleene's Theorem which states the equivalence between three computational models: Regular Expressions, Nondeterministic Finite Automata (NFA), and Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA). Bidirectional arrows show that any RE can be converted to an NFA/DFA and vice versa.
elements: Bidirectional arrow between RE and NFA
Bidirectional arrow between NFA and DFA
Label showing regular languages as the common set
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the fundamental equivalence that defines the class of regular languages - showing that regular expressions and finite automata recognize exactly the same languages

diagrams.title: Proof Construction - NFA to Regular Expression
diagrams.description: A diagram showing one direction of Kleene's proof: converting an NFA to a regular expression using the state elimination method. Shows how states are systematically removed while computing regular expressions for paths between states.
elements: Sample NFA diagram with states q0, q1, q2
Transition arrows with labels (a, b, ε)
State elimination steps shown as progressive transformation
Arrows indicating the conversion process direction
diagrams.purpose: Provides a visual example of the constructive proof showing how any NFA can be algorithmically converted to an equivalent regular expression
